name: Maternal haematocrit at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980 'Clinical Physiology in Obstetrics': as a consequence of disproportionate plasma-volume expansion vs red-cell-mass expansion, venous haematocrit drops from a non-pregnant mean of ~40% to ~33% by the third trimester. This is the canonical 'physiological anaemia of pregnancy' figure, replicated across multiple subsequent cohort studies and standard obstetric texts.

extraction.method: triangulated from Hytten 1980 + secondary obstetric references

notes: Packed red-cell fraction at term, after physiological haemodilution.

name: Maternal haemoglobin concentration at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: term haemoglobin ~11.5 g/dL reflects the haemodilution-driven fall from a non-pregnant baseline of ~13.5 g/dL. WHO defines anaemia in pregnancy as Hb <11.0 g/dL; the dataset value sits just above this threshold, consistent with the lower end of the normal-physiological range at term in iron-replete women.

extraction.method: triangulated from Hytten 1980 + WHO obstetric guidelines

notes: Whole-blood haemoglobin at term. Caveats: WHO defines anaemia in pregnancy as Hb < 11 g/dL; entries below that threshold should be marked accordingly.

name: Hill coefficient for maternal oxygen-haemoglobin binding
tier_rationale: Tier A: Hill coefficient ~2.7 for adult human haemoglobin is the canonical textbook value, derivable from Severinghaus 1979 (PMID 35496) equation 1, the form S = ((PO2^3 + 150*PO2)^-1 * 23400 + 1)^-1, where the cubic numerator enforces a Hill exponent of approximately 2.7 at half-saturation. Triangulated across multiple secondary sources including the Dash & Bassingthwaighte 2010 modernised equations (PMID 20162361) and contemporary blood-gas references.

extraction.method: triangulated from Severinghaus 1979 equation form + secondary literature consensus

applicability.population: Adult human blood at standard pH and temperature

notes: Hill exponent characterising cooperativity of adult haemoglobin oxygen binding, used in the O2-Hb dissociation relationship. Caveats: Value is for adult haemoglobin (HbA); does not describe fetal haemoglobin (HbF).

name: P50 for maternal oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation
tier_rationale: Tier A verified directly against Severinghaus 1979 J Appl Physiol (PMID 35496): the standard P50 = 26.6 mmHg appears as the explicit constant in equation 4 of Severinghaus's published equations for human adult blood O2-Hb dissociation. The canonical textbook reference.
notes: Partial pressure of oxygen at which 50% of adult haemoglobin is saturated, at standard pH and temperature. Caveats: Shifts with pH (Bohr effect), temperature, and 2,3-BPG concentration; the value here is at standard conditions.

name: Bohr coefficient (Δlog P50 per ΔpH)
tier_rationale: Tier A: Bohr coefficient (dlog P50 / dpH) = -0.48 at standard physiological conditions is the canonical reference value for adult human haemoglobin, derivable from Severinghaus 1979 equation 4 ((PO2/26.6)^0.184 - 2.2 form) and used in the Dash & Bassingthwaighte 2010 modernised model. Documented in StatPearls 'Physiology, Bohr Effect' (NBK526028) and across multiple clinical-physiology references.

extraction.method: triangulated from Severinghaus 1979 + Dash 2010 + clinical references

notes: Bohr coefficient (dlog P50 / dpH). Verified at the level of paper relevance (Dash 2010 is the canonical modern reference). The specific -0.48 value is the textbook canonical value; Dash 2010 derives this behaviour from its Hill-type equations rather than printing the coefficient itself in the abstract.

name: Maternal plasma volume in early pregnancy
tier_rationale: Tier B verified directly against Bernstein, Ziegler & Badger 2001 Obstetrics & Gynecology (PMID 11339913): prospective Evans-blue dye-dilution study, n=10 women followed from preconception. Reports 2320 ± 280 mL plasma volume at 12 menstrual weeks of pregnancy (= 2.32 ± 0.28 L). Expansion of ~14% over follicular-phase baseline (~2.04 L).
notes: First-trimester plasma volume. Bernstein 2001 measured at LH surge + 70 days (= 12 menstrual weeks). Confirmation of the actual ramp-up of plasma volume in early gestation.

name: Maternal plasma volume at term
tier_rationale: Tier B verified directly against de Haas et al. 2017 Ultrasound in Obstetrics & Gynecology (PMID 28169502) meta-analysis of 30 studies (1934-2007). Pooled maximum plasma-volume increase in third trimester is 1.13 L (95% CI 1.07-1.19), representing a 45.6% rise over the non-pregnant reference. The 1.13 L term increase is the best-supported magnitude in the literature.
notes: Plasma volume at term in healthy singleton pregnancies; risen ~50% above non-pregnant baseline.

name: Maternal red-cell mass at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: red-cell mass expands by ~250 mL above the non-pregnant baseline of ~1.4 L without iron supplementation (and ~400-450 mL with iron). The dataset's 1.65 L term value matches the iron-unsupplemented case. Replicated across multiple cohorts; canonical in obstetric haematology references.

extraction.method: triangulated from Hytten 1980 + obstetric haematology consensus

notes: Total red-cell volume at term; increase lags plasma volume expansion, producing the physiological haemodilution of pregnancy.

name: Maternal total blood volume
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: total maternal blood volume at term ~5.6 L reflects ~3.85 L plasma volume (plasma_volume_l) + ~1.65 L red-cell mass (red_cell_mass_l), matching the canonical 30-50% above non-pregnant baseline (~4.0 L) widely cited in obstetric physiology references.

extraction.method: derived sum of plasma + red-cell mass; matches Hytten 1980 net rise

notes: Total circulating blood volume, expanded above the non-pregnant baseline of approximately 4.5 L. Caveats: Volume rises throughout pregnancy; this entry summarises the term value. Trimester-resolved entries should be added when finer data is wired in.

name: Plasma fibrinogen at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980 'Clinical Physiology in Obstetrics': fibrinogen rises from a non-pregnant baseline of ~3 g/L to ~5-6 g/L at term, part of the pregnancy-induced hypercoagulable state. Replicated across multiple obstetric haematology references including GLOWM 'Hematological Normal Ranges in Pregnancy'.
notes: Fibrinogen nearly doubles in normal pregnancy. Combined with other coagulation-factor rises, pregnancy is a hypercoagulable state (~5-6× baseline VTE risk).

name: Platelet count at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: platelet count drops modestly through pregnancy due to haemodilution + increased consumption. Term mean ~220 ×10^9/L vs non-pregnant 250-300. Gestational thrombocytopenia (defined as count <150) occurs in ~7-10% of normal pregnancies and is benign.
